claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children I'm asking about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking: How old are each of your children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 20, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Now, let me ask about Alex first, who is 20 years old. Is Alex currently in any approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And does Alex work for 24 hours or more a week, or receive any benefits in their own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work that many hours and doesn't receive any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying about Alex.

Now let me ask about Blake, who is 16. Is Blake currently in any approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake left school recently but has signed up with the local careers service to figure out their next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you, that's helpful information about Blake.

Now let me ask about Charlie, who is 5. Does Charlie live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one more question to complete the assessment:

Are you or anyone else currently receiving Child Benefit for any of these three children - Alex, Blake, or Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody is currently receiving Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's the eligibility assessment for your three children:

**Alex (age 20):** ❌ **Not eligible**
- Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex doesn't meet these criteria, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**Blake (age 16):** ✅ **Eligible for up to 20 weeks**
- Blake has left education but registered with the local careers service. You can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks for a 16 or 17 year old who leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. So you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake during this period.

**Charlie (age 5):** ✅ **Eligible**
- Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you in the UK. You meet the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit as you're responsible for a child under 16 living in the UK.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie (ongoing) and Blake (for up to 20 weeks), but not for Alex.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and is not in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 and not in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has left education but has registered with the local careers service. Under the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since Blake has registered with the careers service, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake for up to 20 weeks.

Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you in the UK. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Since Charlie is 5 and lives with you, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with three children. Charlie (age 5) meets the standard eligibility criteria as a child under 16 living with the claimant. Blake (age 16) is eligible for Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks because they have registered with the careers service after leaving education. Alex (age 20) does not qualify as they are over the age limit and not in approved education or training.

Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 and not in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has left education but has registered with the local careers service. Under the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since Blake has registered with the careers service, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake for up to 20 weeks.

Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you in the UK. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Since Charlie is 5 and lives with you, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with three children. Charlie (age 5) meets the standard eligibility criteria as a child under 16 living with the claimant. Blake (age 16) is eligible for Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks because they have registered with the careers service after leaving education. Alex (age 20) does not qualify as they are over the age limit and not in approved education or training.
